subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree in Aerospace Engineering
Completed undergraduate coursework in aerodynamics, propulsion, aircraft structures, and control systems
Attended a university with a strong aviation or engineering program
Participated in hands-on lab work and design projects during degree
Received additional safety and compliance training required for the aviation industry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Works as an Aeronautic Engineer at Delta Air Lines
Responsibilities include maintenance, repairs, and design of aircraft
Focuses on ensuring aircraft safety, efficiency, and compliance
Supports the development and testing of aircraft systems
Collaborates with multidisciplinary teams for operational readiness

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Employed in the aerospace industry
Specifically works in the commercial aviation sector
Focuses on aircraft design, maintenance, and engineering
Operates within the transportation and logistics domain

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works at Delta Air Lines, a major global airline with over 90,000 employees
Company operates large-scale maintenance and engineering facilities
Work environment combines office, hangar, and on-site aircraft settings
Collaborates with both large engineering teams and cross-functional departments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Delta Air Lines operates in the commercial passenger airline segment
Company provides scheduled air transportation services
Engineering division supports both fleet operations and innovation
Market segment is highly regulated and safety-critical

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3-5 years of professional experience as an aerospace engineer
Joined Delta Air Lines shortly after completing undergraduate degree
Has progressed from junior to mid-level responsibilities within the role
Experience includes exposure to both routine maintenance and design projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an early to mid-career position (equivalent to Engineer I or II)
Acts as a contributing team member rather than a manager
Has increasing autonomy but still works under supervision for critical tasks
Frequently collaborates with more senior engineers and subject matter experts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Designs, develops, and tests aircraft and related systems
Performs regular maintenance checks and troubleshooting on commercial airplanes
Uses CAD and simulation software to model and analyze components
Documents maintenance and design changes in compliance with aviation regulations
Collaborates with other engineers and maintenance teams to resolve technical issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 40% of work hours on design and simulation tasks
Allocates 30% to hands-on maintenance and repair work
Devotes 20% to documentation, compliance checks, and reporting
Uses the remaining 10% for team meetings, training, and process improvement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has autonomy to make technical recommendations within defined parameters
Responsible for routine decision-making in maintenance and design tasks
Requires supervisor approval for major design changes or safety-critical decisions
Can independently troubleshoot and resolve common technical issues
Participates in team-based decision-making for complex projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Using CAD tools for routine component modeling
Performing scheduled maintenance procedures according to established checklists
Interpreting engineering drawings and technical manuals
Documenting standard maintenance actions and updates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Diagnosing unexpected or complex engine failures
Integrating new design elements into existing aircraft systems
Simulating airflow and structural stresses for novel components
Ensuring compliance with evolving regulatory standards

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Reviewing large volumes of maintenance logs for patterns or anomalies
Filling out regulatory compliance forms and checklists
Running routine simulation scenarios with minor parameter changes
Compiling regular reports on aircraft performance metrics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Most enjoyable: Solving complex engineering problems in design or troubleshooting
Engaging: Collaborating on cross-functional teams for aircraft upgrades
Tedious: Entering repetitive data for maintenance documentation
Least enjoyable: Manual review of paperwork and compliance logs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Difficulty quickly identifying root causes of engine problems
Manual data entry for compliance and regulatory requirements
Limited automation in cross-referencing parts and maintenance records
Occasional software compatibility issues between CAD and analysis tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Enjoyment is highest when working on challenging problems that leverage engineering skills
Tasks that match skill level tend to be completed more quickly and accurately
Productivity drops when tasks are overly repetitive or administrative
Learning new tools increases both skill level and engagement, leading to higher productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally enthusiastic about exploring new engineering and AI tools
Believes ongoing learning is essential in aerospace engineering
Sees tech adoption as a way to improve work quality and efficiency
Prefers hands-on experimentation over passive learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new CAD and simulation platforms
Comfortable with iterative learning processes for complex software
Seeks out tutorials and peer guidance when encountering unfamiliar tools
Occasionally requires structured training for major platform changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per month to formal or informal tech training
Participates in company-offered workshops and online courses
Balancing training with workload can be challenging during peak project periods
Prefers bite-sized, on-demand learning resources over lengthy seminars

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the need to stay current in a rapidly evolving field
Wants to use tech to make daily tasks easier and more accurate
Main barriers: high workload and unclear ROI of some new tools
Motivation increases when peers or supervisors endorse a tool's value

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers’ positive experiences with tools influence adoption decisions
Management endorsement accelerates willingness to try new platforms
Regularly exchanges tips and best practices with team members
Company culture generally supports technological experimentation

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Autodesk AutoCAD and SolidWorks for CAD modeling and drafting
Employs CATIA for advanced surface modeling and assemblies
Runs ANSYS Fluent and STAR-CCM+ for computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations
Utilizes Nastran for finite element analysis (FEA) of structural components
Integrates Microsoft Office Suite for documentation and reporting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Hand tools and diagnostic instruments for on-site aircraft maintenance
Standard operating procedures and checklists for compliance
Technical manuals specific to Delta Air Lines’ fleet
Company maintenance management system for scheduling and logging work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with core aerospace engineering software suites (CAD, CFD, FEA)
Understands requirements and limitations of each tool in daily workflow
Familiar with FAA and EASA regulatory documentation systems
Occasionally explores emerging tools through webinars and industry forums

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces file compatibility problems between CATIA and SolidWorks
Data transfer between simulation (CFD/FEA) and CAD tools can require manual adjustment
Managing version control across multiple platforms is sometimes challenging
Some legacy systems are difficult to integrate with newer AI-assisted tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands AI basics such as natural language processing, image recognition, and predictive analytics
Has practical exposure to machine learning concepts within engineering applications
Recognizes differences between rule-based automation and learning algorithms
Keeps up-to-date with AI trends relevant to aerospace engineering

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and other LLM-based assistants for technical queries and documentation
Employs AI modules within ANSYS Fluent for automated mesh generation
Utilizes predictive maintenance analytics tools provided by Delta Air Lines
Occasionally tests AI-powered visual inspection tools for aircraft components

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs daily for drafting reports, troubleshooting, and code generation
Applies AI-driven analytics for early fault detection in engine data
Employs AI image analysis tools for visual inspection pilot projects
Relies on AI suggestions for optimizing design parameters in simulation tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-driven fault detection flagged an early-stage engine issue that was confirmed by manual inspection
Encountered a false positive with AI visual inspection, highlighting the need for human verification
LLM successfully automated parts of maintenance documentation, saving several hours weekly
Observed that AI-generated design suggestions sometimes lacked context-specific constraints

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Company offers periodic workshops on AI and data analytics in engineering
Peers share tips and custom LLM prompts through internal chat forums
Access to online self-paced AI learning modules sponsored by Delta Air Lines
Mentoring available from experienced staff on integrating AI tools into workflow

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers a step-by-step, collaborative approach when using AI for complex tasks
Uses AI independently for routine queries and documentation assistance
Appreciates being able to pause and review AI suggestions before implementation
Occasionally switches to independent use for time-sensitive maintenance issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant or advisor during engineering tasks
Relies on AI as a co-worker for repetitive data analysis and report generation
Prefers to retain final decision-making authority, treating AI as a support tool
Collaborates with AI to brainstorm design alternatives before consulting the team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to experimenting with new AI-driven processes
Advocates for pilot-testing AI tools before full-scale deployment
Willing to adapt personal workflow if AI demonstrates clear value
Seeks feedback from colleagues before integrating new AI tools long-term

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and technical Q&A
Uses command-based interaction for precise simulation or analysis tasks
Finds conversational AI useful for exploring options, but switches to commands for execution
Believes hybrid interfaces offer the most flexibility in engineering contexts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many maintenance and troubleshooting tasks require tacit knowledge from hands-on experience
AI tools are valuable for pattern recognition but may not capture all context-specific nuances
Design decisions often rely on a mix of formal training and practical intuition
Domain knowledge critical for safety and regulatory compliance in aviation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI outputs for initial analysis and documentation preparation
Requires manual review of AI-generated reports before final submission
Skeptical of AI suggestions that lack transparency or explainability
Trust increases when AI results are corroborated by physical inspection or simulation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive data processing and routine analysis
Believes humans should oversee final decisions in maintenance and design for safety
Supports semi-automated workflows where AI proposes and humans verify
Wants AI to handle time-consuming regulatory documentation with human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for non-critical, well-defined tasks (e.g., report generation)
Feels automation is threatening if it eliminates opportunities for skill development
Requires robust override mechanisms for any AI-driven changes affecting safety
Worries about over-reliance on AI for tasks requiring context or judgment

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances analytical and creative engineering skills by reducing manual workload
Sees communication and collaboration skills becoming more important with AI adoption
Worries that routine technical skills may become less valued if fully automated
Feels continuous learning is essential to stay relevant in an AI-augmented environment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to critically evaluate AI-generated outputs for accuracy and relevance
Developing expertise in prompt engineering for LLM-based tools
Participating in pilot projects to integrate AI into engineering workflows
Providing feedback to improve AI tools for aviation-specific applications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about AI automating repetitive documentation and analysis tasks
Does not fear full role replacement due to the need for hands-on and safety-critical work
Believes the human element in troubleshooting and compliance remains indispensable
Views AI as a tool to augment rather than replace skilled engineers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated maintenance recommendations require human signoff
Manual review steps are integrated into AI-driven documentation workflows
Can override or adjust AI-generated simulation parameters before finalizing designs
Company policy mandates human checks for safety-critical AI outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has introduced new guidelines for responsible AI use
Team is encouraged to share feedback on AI tool performance
Policy shifts increasingly support pilot-testing AI before wide rollout
Greater emphasis on transparency and documentation of AI-influenced decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Optimistic that AI will improve safety and efficiency in the aviation industry
Believes AI can help address labor shortages and skills gaps in engineering
Predicts AI will drive new innovations in aircraft design and operations
Recognizes the need for careful management of AI’s societal risks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes transparency and explainability are critical for ethical AI adoption
Worries about bias and data quality issues in AI decision-making
Supports strong governance and oversight for AI in safety-critical roles
Values privacy and data protection in AI-driven workplace tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Wants AI tools that can accurately diagnose complex engine issues in real time
Desires seamless integration of AI-driven insights with existing maintenance dashboards
Would benefit from AI that can cross-validate engineering designs against regulatory standards
Seeks improved natural language interfaces for technical documentation generation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine tasks to become increasingly automated
Anticipates a shift towards more oversight, analysis, and creative problem-solving
Predicts greater collaboration with digital and AI co-workers
Foresees ongoing need for human verification in safety-critical engineering tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Invest in ongoing training and upskilling for engineers in AI and data analytics
Establish clear guidelines for responsible and ethical AI use
Foster cross-functional teams to evaluate and pilot new AI tools
Promote transparency and clear communication about AI’s role in engineering workflows
